common_name: Cherokee Carbon Tomato (Deep-Dark Rich-Flavored Hybrid of Cherokee Purple)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Cherokee Carbon combines the heirloom flavor profile of Cherokee Purple with improved productivity and disease resistance from the Carbon parent — a practical advantage for gardeners who want deep dark tomato flavor with better reliability than pure heirlooms.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Cherokee Carbon is a hybrid cross between two celebrated dark tomatoes — Cherokee Purple and Carbon — that combines the outstanding flavor profile of Cherokee Purple (one of the most beloved dark heirloom tomatoes) with improved productivity, crack resistance, and disease resistance contributed by the Carbon parent. The result is a medium-large (8–12 oz), deep mahogany-to-near-black-brown tomato with characteristic green shoulders and the dense, complex, rich, earthy-sweet flavor that makes dark tomatoes so compelling — a flavor often described as having depth, smokiness, and wine-like complexity quite unlike standard red tomatoes. Cherokee Carbon achieves the flavor combination of its heirloom parent varieties while outperforming both in garden productivity: the plants set more fruit, crack less in wet weather, and show better resistance to soilborne diseases than the pure-heirloom parents. For Gulf South gardeners who have been frustrated by the relatively low productivity and disease susceptibility of pure heirloom dark varieties in the hot, humid zone 9 climate, Cherokee Carbon provides the dark tomato flavor experience with practical garden performance. The deep color results from anthocyanin accumulation in the skin enhanced by sun exposure. In zone 9, grow as a spring crop (February–June) for best fruit development before summer heat.

common_name: Red Shiso (Japanese Purple-Leaf Culinary and Pickling Herb)

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; shiso self-seeds prolifically — allow a few plants to flower and set seed at season end for a naturalized patch that returns reliably without replanting in subsequent years.

directions: Direct sow after the last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; red shiso (Aka Shiso) is the purple-red-leafed variety of the Japanese culinary herb perilla — one of the foundational herbs of Japanese cuisine, used fresh in sushi (wrapped around rice and fish), as a garnish and flavoring for sashimi, in Japanese pickles (umeboshi ume plums pickled with red shiso leaves which provide both color and flavor), and as a tempura vegetable. The deeply colored, crinkled, rounded leaves in vivid burgundy-purple to dark-red-purple create striking ornamental foliage as well as excellent culinary value — red shiso is one of the most ornamentally attractive culinary herbs for the garden. The flavor is uniquely complex: simultaneously minty, anise-like, citrusy, and herbaceous with a spicy undertone that has no direct Western equivalent and is immediately recognizable as distinctly Japanese in character. Red shiso produces its most intense color and flavor in full sun; partial shade lightens the leaf color to red-green but maintains culinary quality. In zone 9, red shiso grows as a warm-season annual (April–October), tolerating Gulf South heat and humidity well. Allow plants to set seed at season end — shiso self-seeds prolifically, establishing a self-renewing naturalized patch that provides leaves season after season without replanting. Fresh young leaves are the primary harvest; flowers are also edible.

common_name: White Cloud Muhly Grass (White-Flowered Gulf Coast Muhly Grass Cultivar)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, lean to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; White Cloud produces its best display in late September through November in the Gulf South — plant in spring for full development by fall bloom time.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; White Cloud is the pure-white-flowered cultivar of Gulf Coast muhly grass (Muhlenbergia capillaris) — producing the same spectacular fall display of airy, cloud-like flower and seed head masses as the standard pink-to-reddish-pink species, but in a pure, soft white rather than the warm pink-magenta of the species. The white flower clouds have a cool, ethereal quality — a mist of white above the fine-textured green foliage — that creates an entirely different aesthetic from the warm, romantic pink of standard muhly: White Cloud works beautifully in white-and-silver garden themes, with blue-violet companions (blue salvias, ageratum, fall asters), or in naturalistic plantings where a softer, more naturalistic tone is desired. The fall bloom season (September–November) is identical to the species — a time when few other native grasses or perennials are at peak display, making White Cloud outstanding for fall garden interest. Like the species, White Cloud is exceptionally drought-tolerant, adapted to full sun, and thrives in poor, lean soils where fertility is minimal and drainage is good. Shear close to the ground in late winter before new growth begins. White Cloud is native in origin, supports a wide range of native seed-eating birds through winter, and requires absolutely minimal maintenance once established.

common_name: Gold Rush Potato (Golden-Flesh Russet-Type Baking Potato)

planting.note: Plant certified seed potato pieces 4–6 weeks before the last frost in loose, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.8–6.5) soil in full sun; Gold Rush is a longer-season potato (90–100 days) — in zone 9, plant as early as January to ensure tubers mature before summer heat arrives; protect young shoots from late frost with row cover.

directions: Plant certified seed potato pieces 4–6 weeks before the last frost in loose, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Gold Rush is a North Dakota State University russet-type potato introduction combining the classic russet potato appearance (oblong, netted-brown skin) with the bright golden-yellow flesh of yellow-flesh varieties — providing a visually distinctive combination of the familiar russet form with surprisingly vivid yellow-gold interior color. The golden-yellow flesh — darker than standard Russet Burbank white flesh and more intense than the pale yellow of Yukon Gold — creates an attractive visual quality in baked, mashed, and roasted preparations where the golden color adds visual appeal to the finished dish. Gold Rush has the high-starch, dry, fluffy interior texture of classic russet-type potatoes — ideal for baking (fluffy, dry-textured baked potato), French fries (crispy exterior, dry interior), and mashed potatoes where a light, dry texture is desired. The russet skin type and long oblong shape produce the classic large baked potato form. In zone 9, Gold Rush's longer season (90–100 days) requires early planting (January) to ensure harvest before summer heat ends the cool potato growing season. Plant deeper than most varieties (6–8 inches) and hill aggressively to promote maximum tuber development in the long cool season before heat forces harvest.

common_name: Galaxy Caladium (White with Vivid Red Spots Fancy-Leaf Caladium)

planting.note: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost when soil has warmed to at least 65°F; plant 1–2 inches deep in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; Galaxy's white-and-red spotted pattern is most vivid in bright partial shade — deep shade may cause the red spots to fade toward pink.

directions: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; Galaxy is a spotted fancy-leaf caladium producing white to cream-white heart-shaped leaves decorated with vivid red to crimson spots and splashes scattered across the leaf surface, creating a constellation-like pattern that gives the variety its evocative name. The contrasting pattern of vivid red spots on a white background creates one of the most visually energetic caladium patterns — the red-on-white spotted effect is immediately striking and distinctive, quite different from the veined or marbled patterns of most other caladium varieties. The irregular spot placement — no two leaves have identical pattern — contributes a spontaneous, natural quality to the display that complements both formal and informal garden styles. Galaxy adds a unique spotted pattern to mixed caladium plantings where it creates visual variety alongside the solid-colored, veined, and bicolor types. In zone 9, Galaxy performs reliably through the entire warm season (May–October) with tubers increasing in size year to year in-ground plantings. The white background and red spots create outstanding combinations with solid green groundcovers (mondo grass, liriope) and dark companions (black ajuga, dark heuchera) where the spotted pattern provides maximum contrast. Maintain consistent moisture and mulch to ensure the finest leaf quality.
